mnemonics.title: PKI Components
mnemonics.content: CARRS: CA (issues), RA (registers), Repository (stores), Revocation (CRL/OCSP), Subscribers (end entities)

mnemonics.title: X.509 Certificate Fields
mnemonics.content: SAIV: Serial number, Algorithm, Issuer, Validity period

mnemonics.title: Certificate Chain Verification
mnemonics.content: Verify UP: User trusts Root CA → Path building → Policy checking → Signature verification → Validity checking → Revocation status

acronyms.usage: Framework for creating, managing, distributing, and revoking digital certificates
Trusted third party that issues and verifies digital certificates
Entity that verifies identity of certificate requestors before CA issues certificates
List of revoked certificates published by CA
Real-time protocol for checking certificate revocation status
Request sent to CA to obtain a digital certificate
Document defining rules for certificate issuance and usage
Document describing CA's practices for issuing certificates

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a ladder where each rung validates the one above it. The Root CA is at the top (ground), intermediate CAs in the middle, and your certificate at the bottom. You trust the ladder because the top is firmly anchored (trusted root).

Think of a mailbox: the public key is the mailbox slot (anyone can drop letters in), and the private key is the only key that opens the box to read them. You share the slot freely but guard the key jealously.

Imagine a wax seal on a letter. The sender uses their private 'seal' (key) to create a unique pattern. Anyone with the matching 'stamp' (public key) can verify it's genuinely from them and wasn't opened.
CRL is like a published blacklist of cancelled credit cards - you check the list. OCSP is like calling the bank in real-time to ask 'is this card valid?' Both achieve the same goal but differently.

analogies.analogy: PKI works like a government issuing ID cards (certificates). The government (Root CA) is implicitly trusted. It issues ID cards through authorized agencies (Intermediate CAs). If an ID is revoked, it's like the government issuing a 'wanted' notice (CRL).
A digital certificate is like a passport: it proves your identity (binds key to identity), is issued by a trusted authority (CA/government), has an expiration date, and can be revoked if compromised.

A certificate chain is like proving you're part of a royal family: you show your birth certificate (your cert), your parents' cert, grandparents' cert, all the way to the founding ancestor (Root CA). If any link is broken or untrusted, the claim fails.
A Certificate Authority is like a notary public: it verifies identities, stamps documents with its official seal (digital signature), and is trusted by society to tell the truth about who owns what.
